key_framework: OECD AI Principles
implications: Widely used baseline for trustworthy AI (robustness, transparency, accountability, human-centred values); cross-border policy anchor for enterprise AI governance.

key_framework: Sound Practices for Responsible Adoption of AI; Financial Stability Implications of AI
implications: Pushes institution-wide AI governance across the AI lifecycle, board/senior-management focus, third-party/vendor dependence, and financial-stability vulnerabilities.

key_framework: BIS/FSI work on regulating AI in finance; BCBS work on AI/ML, model risk, operational resilience, third-party risk
implications: Treats AI as an extension of prudential governance and model risk management - explainability, validation, data governance, accountability in core banking use cases.

key_framework: Work on AI in capital markets and conduct/market-integrity implications
implications: Relevant for broker-dealers, exchanges, asset managers using AI in advice, surveillance, market-abuse detection, investor comms; expect conduct, transparency, market-integrity controls.

key_framework: Insurance supervisory work applying AI governance, model and consumer-protection expectations
implications: Important for bancassurance/insurance affiliates - underwriting fairness, explainability, governance, customer outcomes.

key_framework: AI use in AML/CFT under risk-based compliance frameworks
implications: Supports AI-enabled surveillance and fraud detection but requires explainable controls, data quality, and oversight where AI influences suspicious-activity monitoring.

key_framework: EU AI Act (Reg. (EU) 2024/1689) plus sectoral financial rules
implications: High-risk AI use cases require stronger controls on data governance, human oversight, logging, monitoring, documentation; map use cases against both AI Act and sector rules.

key_framework: Existing prudential governance, model risk, outsourcing, operational resilience applied to AI
implications: Significant institutions should expect AI reviewed through governance, internal controls, and model-risk lenses rather than as a standalone carve-out.

key_framework: AI Update (2024); existing rules apply (Consumer Duty, SM&CR, operational resilience); sandbox/innovation pathways
implications: Firms remain accountable under conduct, Consumer Duty, operational resilience and SM&CR-style governance even when using third-party or embedded AI tools.

key_framework: SS1/23 Model Risk Management Principles and operational resilience applied to AI/ML
implications: Prudentially important firms should classify many AI systems as model-risk-bearing, requiring validation, controls, clear ownership.

key_framework: AI and data protection guidance
implications: Any AI using personal data needs lawful basis, transparency, minimisation, and governance around automated decision-making and fairness.

key_framework: SR 26-2 (Apr 2026) Model Risk Management - supersedes SR 11-7; safety-and-soundness expectations extended to AI/ML
implications: Treat AI systems used for material decisions as governed models requiring validation, change management, bias review, vendor oversight. SR 26-2 explicitly extends MRM discipline to AI/ML.

key_framework: Guidance on AI in investment advice / broker-dealer activity; Predictive Data Analytics proposal; AI-washing enforcement focus
implications: Broker-dealers/advisers may need to identify and neutralise AI-related conflicts in investor interactions; AI marketing claims must be supportable.

key_framework: AI topic hub; Regulatory Notice 24-09; guidance on AI in customer-facing communications
implications: Treats AI as subject to existing technology-neutral rules - communications, supervision, surveillance, suitability-style processes, third-party tools.

key_framework: Existing consumer financial protection and automated-decisioning scrutiny
implications: AI affecting retail credit, servicing or chatbots can trigger UDAAP, adverse-action, fair-lending and transparency concerns.

key_framework: Unfair/deceptive practices, automated decision-making, AI claims
implications: AI claims, explainability, fairness and deceptive product statements create enforcement exposure, especially for client-facing features and marketing.

key_framework: AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, Jan 2023)
implications: Not a financial regulator but a strong control taxonomy for mapping policy, testing, monitoring and governance requirements into an enterprise framework.

key_framework: FEAT principles; Veritas methodology; AI Risk Management Guidelines (consultation Nov 2025, finalisation expected 2026); AI Verify; Notice FSM-N20
implications: Strong benchmark for fairness, ethics, accountability, transparency in financial AI; more prescriptive than UK/AU; mandatory inventory and lifecycle controls proposed.

key_framework: High-level AI principles; consumer protection / regtech / supervisory expectations
implications: Important for chatbot, regtech, fraud and operational AI deployments; emphasis on governance and implementation discipline.

key_framework: Existing conduct and algorithmic controls applied to AI in markets; GenAI language-model circular
implications: Securities firms should expect AI oversight in trading, client communications, surveillance and outsourcing governance.

key_framework: Generative AI, recommendation algorithms, deep synthesis, and sector supervision
implications: One of the more prescriptive regimes - content, security, algorithm filing and sector controls that can materially affect model deployment architecture.

key_framework: Existing financial regulation plus AI governance guidance references
implications: Typically principles-based, but material AI use still raises governance, outsourcing and explainability expectations.

key_framework: APRA 30 Apr 2026 AI industry letter (expects ISO/IEC 42001 & 23894, integrated assurance); CPS 230, CPS 234, CPG 234 (extended to AI), CPS 220; ASIC 8 May 2026 cyber-resilience letter
implications: Most explicit prudential statement on AI to date. Integrated assurance across cyber, data governance, model performance, operational resilience, privacy, conduct. AI governance failures treated as licence/duty-of-care issues.

key_framework: Emerging AI governance in finance noted in comparative supervisory work
implications: Relevant for regional banking operations; likely focus is governance, outsourcing, data handling, prudent deployment.

key_framework: Financial free-zone AI/ML guidance plus national AI governance developments
implications: Important for cross-border hubs using AI in wealth, markets and compliance tooling, especially outsourcing and data-transfer issues.

key_framework: AI governed through prudential, consumer, privacy and securities rules; OSFI Guideline E-23 (model risk)
implications: AI in banking and wealth products should be controlled through model risk, outsourcing, privacy and fair-outcomes frameworks.

key_framework: AI governance emerging alongside data protection (LGPD) and financial supervision
implications: Material for Latin America operations, especially customer data, model fairness and prudential accountability.

key_framework: Existing financial rules and data/privacy law applied to AI
implications: Relevant for conduct, model governance and outsourcing in bank and insurer use cases.

key_framework: Existing sectoral regulation plus digital/AI governance developments
implications: AI in lending, onboarding, fraud and capital markets raises explainability, outsourcing and fairness issues even absent a dedicated financial AI rulebook.

key_framework: Technology-neutral prudential and outsourcing supervision; AI risk-management guidance
implications: Important for cross-border wealth and banking operations; focus on accountability, model governance, data risk and resilience.
